Source_Species.reason: The study directly tests rat aortas and human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs), i.e., rat and human systems.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Methods specify Sprague-Dawley rats and HUVECs, which correspond to Rattus norvegicus and Homo sapiens.

Applied_Species.reason: Results and methods explicitly use rats in vivo and human endothelial cells (HUVECs) in vitro.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: These applied species correspond to Rattus norvegicus and Homo sapiens as stated/implied by rat and HUVEC use.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: Results section 2.4 and 2.7 state that SIRT1 levels associated with eNOS decreased in aging/d-gal groups and were restored by treatment; eNOS immunoprecipitation was used to assess this association.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: eNOS is the endothelial nitric oxide synthase discussed throughout the paper; this places it within the nitric oxide synthase family.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show mulberry/anthocyanins increased eNOS Ser1177 phosphorylation, decreased Thr495 phosphorylation in rat aorta, reduced eNOS acetylation, and restored NO production in rats and HUVECs.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: NO is repeatedly measured and discussed as the direct product linked to eNOS function.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: NO is indeed a gaseous signaling molecule, but the paper does not explicitly use the phrase 'gaseous signaling metabolite'.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Figure 2 reports NO in rat serum and aorta; Figure 6 reports NO release and intracellular NO in HUVECs.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Section 2.2 states endothelium-derived NO is important for maintaining normal vascular function; the paper links restored NO to improved endothelial function.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed approaches are present: Western blot for phospho/total eNOS, aortic immunofluorescence for p-eNOS Ser1177, eNOS immunoprecipitation with acetyl-lysine blot, Griess/DAF-2DA NO assays, and peroxynitrite/nitrotyrosine measurements.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: The paper explicitly examines aging rats, d-galactose-induced HUVEC senescence, mulberry extract, cyanidin-3-rutinoside (C-3-R), and cyanidin-3-glucoside (C-3-G).

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Methods identify male Sprague-Dawley rats and HUVECs.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each point is reflected in Results: aging/d-gal lowered eNOS activation and increased acetylation; anthocyanins/mulberry restored phosphorylation and reduced acetylation; NO increased while peroxynitrite/nitrotyrosine decreased.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper centers mechanistically on eNOS post-translational regulation (phosphorylation/acetylation) and does not report eNOS transcriptional analysis.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Figure 3 and Figure 6 describe altered phosphorylation state with total eNOS measured; Discussion explicitly notes mulberry did not alter total eNOS expression.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Methods specify Sprague-Dawley rats and HUVECs as the biological sources used.

Source_Species.reason: The study tests SIRT1 in rat aorta and human HUVECs.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Rat and HUVEC correspond to Rattus norvegicus and Homo sapiens.

Applied_Species.reason: SIRT1 was examined in rats and human endothelial cells (HUVECs) in the Results.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied systems are rat and human HUVECs, i.e., Rattus norvegicus and Homo sapiens.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: Results state SIRT1 levels associated with eNOS were altered and restored, showing interaction/association with eNOS.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper describes SIRT1 as a sirtuin whose activity depends on NAD+/NADH context and mediates deacetylation; this supports identification as an NAD+-dependent sirtuin deacetylase.

Regulator_Type.reason: SIRT1 is presented as a deacetylase that regulates endothelial signaling/metabolism via eNOS deacetylation.

Regulation_Mode.reason: Sections 2.4 and 2.7 explicitly discuss SIRT1-mediated deacetylation of eNOS and positive effects on eNOS activity.

Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes.reason: The deacetylation of eNOS and association with increased eNOS activity/NO are supported, but the field says 'target genes'; the paper tests protein-level regulation of eNOS, not regulation of genes.

Primary_Regulatory_Targets.reason: eNOS is the main downstream target examined for SIRT1 association/deacetylation in this study.

Metabolic_Process_Controlled.reason: The study links SIRT1 restoration to eNOS deacetylation, NO production, and reduced oxidative/nitrosative stress, supporting control of nitric oxide biosynthesis and endothelial redox homeostasis.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show anthocyanin/mulberry restored SIRT1 expression/activity, reduced eNOS acetylation, increased NO, and reduced senescence/oxidative stress markers.

Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product.reason: The paper supports SIRT1 as a positive regulator of NO bioavailability through eNOS deacetylation, but calling it 'decisive' is stronger than directly demonstrated because no SIRT1 loss/gain-of-function experiment was performed in this study.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: NO is the metabolite whose bioavailability is linked to SIRT1-eNOS regulation in the Results.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: While NO is a gaseous signaling molecule, that exact classification phrase is not used in the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper explicitly links NO with maintenance of vascular endothelial function and reduced endothelial dysfunction.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are present in the paper: SIRT1 Western blot, sirtuin activity, NAD+/NADH ratio, eNOS immunoprecipitation with acetyl-lysine/SIRT1 blotting, NO assays, and rat/HUVEC models.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: These factors are all directly used in the experiments: aged rats, d-gal-induced HUVEC senescence, mulberry extract, C-3-R, and C-3-G.

Protein_Complex_Involvement.reason: The paper states SIRT1 is physically associated with eNOS and measures SIRT1 associated with eNOS after immunoprecipitation.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Methods identify Sprague-Dawley rats and HUVECs.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: The biological materials used are Sprague-Dawley rats and HUVECs.

Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs.reason: Anthocyanin-rich mulberry extract, C-3-R, C-3-G, aging/d-gal status, and NAD+/NADH ratio are all experimentally linked to SIRT1 status in the paper.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Results explicitly state SIRT1 expression/activity were reduced in aging rat aorta and d-gal-treated HUVECs and restored by anthocyanin/mulberry treatment.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper suggests vascular-health relevance of anthocyanin interventions, but it does not discuss breeding or engineering applications; the caveat 'not a crop breeding target in this paper' is an external interpretation.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Accurate to the paper: SIRT1 is presented as a mechanistic mediator, and the study does not perform direct genetic manipulation of SIRT1.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: These three summary points match Results sections 2.4 and 2.7 on reduced SIRT1/NAD+/NADH in aging/senescence, restoration by anthocyanins/mulberry, and association with lower eNOS acetylation, higher NO, and improved endothelial status.
